result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Attacker
    participant Initiator
    participant Responder
    participant Server
    participant SignReg
    participant EncReg

    alt Trace for secretA
        Attacker->>Initiator: xA = B
        Attacker->>Server: (B, B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((B, spk(sskB)), skS), sign((B, pk(skB)), skS)
        Attacker->>SignReg: (a2, a4)
        Note right of SignReg: insert sign_keys(a2, a4)
        Attacker->>EncReg: (B, pk(a6))
        Note right of EncReg: insert enc_keys(B, pk(a6))
        Attacker->>Server: (a2, B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((a2, a4), skS), sign((B, pk(a6)), skS)
        Attacker->>Initiator: cert1 = sign((B, spk(sskB)), skS), cert2 = sign((B, pk(a6)), skS)
        Initiator-->>Attacker: (cert1, cert2, encrypt(sign((B, B, k), sskB), pk(a6)))
        Initiator-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretA, k)

    alt Trace for secretB
        Attacker->>Responder: xB = B
        Attacker->>SignReg: (a1, a5)
        Note right of SignReg: insert sign_keys(a1, a5)
        Attacker->>EncReg: (A, spk(a9))
        Note right of EncReg: insert enc_keys(A, spk(a9))
        Attacker->>Server: (a1, A)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((a1, a5), skS), sign((A, spk(a9)), skS)
        Attacker->>SignReg: (a3, a7)
        Note right of SignReg: insert sign_keys(a3, a7)
        Attacker->>Server: (a3, B)
        Server-->>Attacker: sign((a3, a7), skS), sign((B, pk(skB)), skS)
        Attacker->>Responder: cert1 = sign((A, spk(a9)), skS), cert2 = sign((B, pk(skB)), skS), km = encrypt(sign((A, B, y), a9), pk(skB))
        Responder-->>Attacker: sencrypt(secretB, y)
    end
result.explanation: Both secrecy queries fail because the model lets the attacker populate enc_keys for any host through process_encK. Unlike process_signK, there is no check excluding A and B. The server then signs attacker-controlled encryption-key entries as if they were valid certificates for honest hosts. In the secretA trace, the attacker registers enc_keys(B, pk(attacker)), gets a server-signed certificate for B, and makes the initiator encrypt the session material to the attacker's key, which reveals k and then secretA. In the secretB trace, the attacker again abuses enc_keys and the responder accepts a server-signed value sign((A, spk(a9)), skS) as cert1, so the attacker-chosen key a9 is used to validate the inner signed message and secretB is released under attacker-known y.
result.notes: The secretB trace also shows certificate confusion: a server-signed term of the form sign((A, spk(a9)), skS) is accepted in the responder's cert1 position. The artifacts support this behavior, but the clearest minimal fix directly evidenced by both attacks is to stop attacker-controlled enc_keys entries for honest hosts.
